channel.title: DevLens — New Concepts
channel.description: Newly added concepts on DevLens, a free static knowledge base for working developers.

item.title: Async/Await Pitfalls
item.description: The classic async/await traps in C#: blocking with .Result/.Wait() causing deadlocks, async void, fire-and-forget, and missing ConfigureAwait — and how to avoid each.

item.title: Cancellation & Timeouts
item.description: How cooperative cancellation works in .NET with CancellationToken: passing tokens through async calls, enforcing timeouts, linking tokens, and why cancellation is cooperative, not forced.

item.title: Channels & Producer/Consumer
item.description: How System.Threading.Channels gives you a safe async producer/consumer pipeline with built-in backpressure — decoupling fast producers from slower consumers without manual locking.

item.title: Concurrency vs Parallelism
item.description: Concurrency is structuring work so tasks can make progress independently; parallelism is literally running them at the same time on multiple cores — related but not the same.

item.title: Locks & Race Conditions
item.description: Why concurrent access to shared mutable state corrupts it, and how locks (mutual exclusion) fix it — plus the costs: contention, deadlocks, and why you minimize the critical section.

item.title: Thread Safety & Atomicity
item.description: What makes code thread-safe: atomic operations, immutability, confinement, and concurrent collections — and why visibility (memory barriers) matters as much as mutual exclusion.

item.title: Integration Testing
item.description: Why integration tests catch the bugs unit tests can't — wiring, serialization, SQL, configuration — and how to write them realistically with in-memory hosts and real dependencies in containers.

item.title: Mocking & Test Doubles
item.description: The family of test doubles — dummy, stub, spy, mock, fake — how they isolate the unit under test from slow or nondeterministic dependencies, and the trap of over-mocking.

item.title: Test-Driven Development
item.description: Test-Driven Development: the red-green-refactor loop, writing the failing test first to drive design, and why TDD is as much a design discipline as a testing one.

item.title: The Test Pyramid
item.description: The test pyramid: many fast unit tests at the base, fewer integration tests in the middle, and a handful of slow end-to-end tests at the top — and why inverting it (the ice-cream cone) hurts.

item.title: Unit Testing
item.description: What a good unit test looks like: testing one unit in isolation, the Arrange-Act-Assert structure, the FIRST properties, and testing behavior rather than implementation.

item.title: Branching Strategies
item.description: The main branching models — Git Flow, GitHub Flow, and trunk-based development — and how branch lifetime and merge frequency trade off against integration pain and release cadence.

item.title: How Git Works: Objects & Refs
item.description: Git's data model demystified: content-addressed blobs, trees and commits, branches as movable pointers, and why a commit is an immutable snapshot in a directed acyclic graph.

item.title: Merge vs Rebase
item.description: Merge preserves history with a merge commit; rebase rewrites commits onto a new base for a linear history. When to use each, and the golden rule: never rebase shared/public branches.

item.title: Resolving Merge Conflicts
item.description: Why merge conflicts happen, how to read the conflict markers, the step-by-step resolution workflow, and how to prevent conflicts by integrating early and keeping changes small.

item.title: Undoing Changes: reset, revert, reflog
item.description: The right tool for each undo: revert to safely undo a public commit, reset to move a branch (and the soft/mixed/hard modes), and reflog to recover commits you thought you lost.

item.title: CI/CD Pipelines
item.description: What continuous integration and continuous delivery/deployment actually mean: the automated build → test → deploy pipeline, fast feedback, and shipping small changes often.

item.title: Containers & Images
item.description: How container images package an app with its dependencies into a portable, immutable, layered artifact — and why 'build once, run anywhere' fixes the works-on-my-machine problem.

item.title: Deployment Strategies
item.description: How to release without downtime or big-bang risk: rolling, blue-green, and canary deployments — and how each trades infrastructure cost against rollback speed and blast radius.

item.title: Feature Flags
item.description: Feature flags decouple deploying code from releasing a feature — letting you ship dark, roll out gradually, run experiments, and kill a bad feature instantly without a redeploy.

item.title: Infrastructure as Code
item.description: Managing servers, networks, and cloud resources as version-controlled, declarative code — so infrastructure is reproducible, reviewable, and free of manual 'snowflake' drift.

item.title: Authentication vs Authorization
item.description: The core distinction every security discussion rests on: authentication proves who you are; authorization decides what you're allowed to do — two separate steps, often confused.

item.title: Cross-Site Scripting (XSS)
item.description: How XSS injects attacker JavaScript into your pages (stored, reflected, DOM-based), what it lets attackers do, and the defenses: output encoding, Content Security Policy, and sanitization.

item.title: Encryption Basics
item.description: Symmetric vs asymmetric encryption, what each is good for, how TLS combines them, and the difference between encryption in transit and at rest — without rolling your own crypto.

item.title: Hashing & Salting
item.description: Why passwords are hashed not encrypted, what a salt defends against (rainbow tables), and why a slow password hash (bcrypt/Argon2) beats a fast one like SHA-256.

item.title: SQL Injection
item.description: How SQL injection works — untrusted input concatenated into a query becomes executable SQL — and the one real fix: parameterized queries that separate code from data.

item.title: Alerting & Dashboards
item.description: How to alert on symptoms not causes, page only on actionable user-impacting conditions, avoid alert fatigue, and design dashboards that answer 'is it healthy?' at a glance.

item.title: Distributed Tracing & Spans
item.description: How distributed tracing follows one request across services as a tree of timed spans, how trace context propagates between hops, and why it's the only signal that localizes latency in a distributed call.

item.title: Metrics, SLIs & SLOs
item.description: How to measure reliability that matters: the golden signals, SLIs (what you measure), SLOs (the target), SLAs (the contract), and error budgets that balance reliability against shipping speed.

item.title: Structured Logging
item.description: Why logging key-value structured events beats plain text strings: machine-queryable fields, message templates, correlation IDs, and appropriate log levels — so logs are searchable, not just readable.

item.title: The Three Pillars: Logs, Metrics, Traces
item.description: The three telemetry signals that make a system observable — logs (events), metrics (aggregates), and traces (request journeys) — and which question each one answers.

item.title: Arrays vs Linked Lists
item.description: The fundamental trade-off between contiguous arrays (O(1) indexing, cache-friendly) and linked lists (O(1) insert/delete given the node) — and why arrays usually win in practice.

item.title: Big-O Cheat Sheet
item.description: A scannable reference of time and space complexities: common data-structure operations (array, hash table, BST, heap) and the major sorting algorithms, with best/average/worst cases.

item.title: Big-O Notation
item.description: What Big-O actually measures — how an algorithm's time or space grows with input size — the common classes from O(1) to O(n!), and why we care about growth, not stopwatch time.

item.title: Hash Tables
item.description: How hash tables give O(1) average lookup by mapping keys to buckets, how collisions are handled (chaining vs open addressing), and why the worst case is O(n) and load factor matters.

item.title: Sorting Algorithms
item.description: Why comparison sorts can't beat O(n log n), how quicksort/mergesort/heapsort differ in practice (speed, space, stability), and when non-comparison sorts (counting/radix) apply.

item.title: Stacks & Queues
item.description: Two restricted sequences that show up everywhere: the stack (LIFO — undo, recursion, parsing) and the queue (FIFO — scheduling, BFS, buffering), both with O(1) push/pop.

item.title: Trees & Graphs
item.description: The non-linear structures behind huge swaths of problems: trees (hierarchies, BSTs, heaps) and graphs (networks), plus the two traversals — DFS and BFS — that power most algorithms on them.

item.title: API Versioning
item.description: How to evolve an API without breaking existing clients: versioning strategies (URL, header, media type), backward-compatible additive changes, and deprecation policy.

item.title: Service Mesh
item.description: How a service mesh moves cross-cutting networking concerns — retries, mTLS, traffic shifting, observability — out of application code into sidecar proxies managed by a control plane.

item.title: Strangler Fig Pattern
item.description: How to migrate off a legacy monolith incrementally and safely: route slices of functionality to new services behind a façade, growing the new system as the old one shrinks — no big-bang rewrite.

item.title: Boxing & Unboxing
item.description: What happens when a value type is treated as an object — it's boxed onto the heap — why that costs allocations and GC pressure, and how generics let you avoid it.

item.title: Records
item.description: How C# records give you concise, immutable reference types with value-based equality, non-destructive mutation via with-expressions, and built-in deconstruction — ideal for DTOs and domain values.

item.title: Value vs Reference Types
item.description: The core C# type distinction: value types (structs) copied by value and usually stack/inline-allocated, vs reference types (classes) copied by reference and heap-allocated — and where each lives.
